News.title: Mum-to-be wins £70k pay-out after boss made bogus claims & sacked her for working from home due to morning sickness
News.content:
BABY TROUBLE Mum-to-be wins £70k pay-out after boss made bogus claims & sacked her for working from home due to morning sickness

A VIDEO editor has won £70,000 in a pregnancy discrimination case after her boss sacked her in a row over “severe” morning sickness.

Mum-to-be Kaila Farmer angered her boss, Harry Pill, after producing a doctor’s note saying she had to work from home because of her nausea and vomiting, a tribunal heard.



Mr Pill had previously made comments to other staff about how maternity pay was “unfair”, it was heard.



After Ms Farmer’s announcement, he congratulated her but secretly plotted to fire her over bogus allegations that her performance was not good enough.

He was “clearly unhappy” she could not work in their Oxford office and had to work from home, a tribunal ruled.

Ms Farmer has now successfully sued his company Fresh Cut Video for £73,500 for unfair dismissal and pregnancy discrimination.

Ms Farmer joined Fresh Cut Video in January 2023 as a video editor and operations manager.



She initially worked remotely for the Oxford-based company because she was living in South Africa, but she moved to Reading, Berks, in October 2023 to live with her husband, who had been in the UK since 2020.

They planned to start a family, it was heard.

Now she was based in the UK, Ms Farmer was expected to work in Fresh Cut Video’s office in Oxford.

Talented Ms Farmer received bonuses for her work and at her first probation meeting Mr Pill told her “you are doing great”.



Between January 8 and 16 of 2024, Ms Farmer was working from home as she was sick.

She was due to be in the office to take part in an important call on the afternoon of January 16 but reached out to Mr Pill in the morning to say she would be unable to make it.

When she suggested she could still join the meeting remotely, Mr Pill said: “Hi Kaila, no problem… don’t worry about the meeting.”

In light of her boss telling not to worry about it, she confirmed that she would be too sick to join and he replied: “Sorry to hear you are unwell. Really hope you feel better tomorrow.”

That day, Mr Pill complained to his mother – who managed HR for the company – saying that Ms Farmer “doesn’t care about me at all”.



He texted Mrs Pill: “Time to check about that probation… not noticing/caring an emergency like that call and telling me about it today at 7:20am instead of last night – doesn’t care about me at all.”

The next day, Ms Farmer told Mr Pill that she was “excited” to tell him that she was pregnant but that her doctor had advised her to work from home.

Mr Pill “appeared to receive the news positively” and said it was “fine” to work from home.

She then emailed him saying: “Hey Harry, as per our conversation earlier I just wanted to write this out to you to formalise as per the GP’s instructions.

“I am pregnant and expecting to give birth at the end of July/early August 2024.



“Because of my age and severe nausea and vomiting, the GP has recommended that I keep all stress and commuting low and work from home until my nausea and vomiting subsides (could be six weeks).”

That evening, Mr Pill’s mother sent the company’s HR and employment law firm Peninsula an email which read: “We have consistently made it very clear to KF that the post is office-based with some work on location but managing the commute has been an ongoing challenge for KF.

“In the meeting today, KF advised Harry that she is pregnant and has some additional health challenges that will necessitate her working from home for the time being.”

A week later on January 23 Fresh Cut Video formally instructed Peninsula to advise them for an upcoming performance review of Ms Farmer.

That day, Ms Farmer downloaded work files onto her personal computer which was “usual” and which Mr Pill had known about – but on this occasion he claimed it was a “data breach” and “stealing data”.



Ms Farmer was locked out of the company systems and invited to a probationary review meeting – although the invitation warned: “I must advise you that the possible outcomes following this meeting include termination of your employment.”

She asked to postpone the meeting on health grounds but was denied and the meeting commenced in her absence.

On February 19, pregnant Ms Farmer was dismissed for poor performance and alleged misconduct by way of a termination letter.

In an earlier email to Peninsula, Mr Pill suggested he knew he was going to fire Mr Farmer by saying: “I think it might be a simpler process for everybody if she could just work from home for these last few days whilst she’s with us.”

Ms Farmer sued the company at Reading Employment Tribunal, where Employment Judge Christabel McCooey found that her claims of unfair dismissal and discrimination based on her pregnancy succeeded.



Judge McCooey said based from the messages to HR firm Peninsula, Fresh Cut Video was “clearly unhappy” Ms Farmer would not be in the office.

Judge McCooey said: “Pregnancy is explicitly mentioned in the context of the main concern, which is the post being office-based and of [Ms Farmer] taking sick leave.

“I therefore infer that the concerns about the role being office-based were significantly influenced and exacerbated by the disclosure of the pregnancy and [her] current sick leave related to that pregnancy, which was set to last for at least six weeks.”

Judge McCooey added: “I find Mr Pill tended to downplay his concerns to [Ms Farmer], giving the impression that things were fine with him when, in reality, they were not.

“[Emails Mr Pill sent] suggest the decision to dismiss [Ms Farmer] was predetermined prior to the February 6 2025 and prior to receiving the report from Peninsula. This undermines Mr Pill’s credibility when it comes to looking at the real reason for [Ms Farmer’s] dismissal.



“I therefore conclude that Mr Pill emphasised performance issues as a justification to dismiss the [Ms Farmer], when in reality, her pregnancy and related sick leave was the principal reason for her dismissal.”

The judge continued: “The performance and conduct concerns raised by [Fresh Cut Video] do not singularly or collectively amount to a convincing explanation as to why it dismissed [Ms Farmer] for reasons unconnected to her pregnancy.”

RECOMMENDED STORIES

A further claim of failure to deal with a flexible working request was dismissed.

The tribunal found Mr Pill had previously said to another colleague that maternity pay was “unfair”.

News.title: Rachel Reeves slammed for ‘gaslighting’ over defence spending as Tories warn Britain is being left dangerously exposed
News.content:
THE Chancellor has been accused of gaslighting over defence spending — with furious Tories warning Britain is being left dangerously exposed.

Rachel Reeves insisted she is delivering “the ­biggest uplift since the Cold War” and pointed to a £1billion helicopter deal securing 3,300 jobs.



But Tory former minister Dame Caroline Dinenage raged the Armed Forces are being told to tighten belts while global threats soar.



She fumed: “This is the first year since the 1980s that we haven’t had a ship in the Gulf – at a time when the Middle East is a tinderbox.

“The RFA (Royal Fleet Auxiliary) is on its knees. Defence companies are being tied up with bureaucracy, dither and delay.

“She’s gaslighting the British people.

“This is a disaster.”



Meanwhile, senior Tory Sir Julian Lewis warned Ms Reeves she cannot rely on post-Cold War spending levels when “we’re in a hot war in Europe and an incendiary war in the Middle East.”

The Chancellor’s clash with Tory MPs came after senior military chiefs warned of a £28billion gap in defence funding and called for an urgent rise in spending.

Ms Reeves, however, delivered a deliberately low-key Spring Statement with no new defence cash.

UK defence spending is forecast to reach 2.5 per cent of GDP by 2027, but there is still no timetable for hitting the Government’s 3 per cent target.

News.title: Terrified Brits trapped in Middle East offered escape route home to UK… if they can squeeze onto single 350-seater jet
News.content:
TERRIFIED Brits trapped in the Middle East have been offered an escape route home – on a single charter jet with 350 seats.

Up to 132,000 stranded Brits have already registered their presence in the region amid the rapidly escalating conflict between the US and Israel against Iran.





As many as 110,000 UK citizens have been sheltering from waves of Iranian drones and missiles in the United Arab Emirates across the past four days.

It has now been revealed that only one government jet, with the capacity of around 350 passengers, is set to be sent out to the Middle East immediately.

Foreign Secretary Yvette Cooper said the solitary mercy flight would take off for Muscat, Oman, to bring Brits home by the weekend.



Those in the UAE lucky enough to get a seat will have to travel 300 miles by road to get it.

It comes as…

Those left behind have been advised to continue monitoring travel advice and contact their airlines.

The announcement is likely to spark fury from those stranded after America urged its citizens to leave the Middle East immediately.



A handful of commercial flights are already bringing thousands of stranded Britons back to the UK.

But the vast majority of departures from the region have been cancelled after the airspace was closed.

Ms Cooper told MPs it was “a very fast-moving situation” with “unprecedented numbers of British nationals in the region”.

She added: “We are working with airlines on increasing capacity out of Muscat for British nationals.

“A Government charter flight will fly from Muscat in the coming days, prioritising vulnerable nationals.”



A Foreign Office spokesperson said: “We’re asking all British nationals to follow our travel advice, which is changing very quickly.

“In relation to US travel advice, US nationals are assessed to be subject to a particular threat because of the nature of the US involvement in this conflict.

“We have very carefully reviewed the threat to British nationals on an hourly basis inside the crisis centre.

“We update travel advice very regularly and we’ll continue to do that based on our assessment of the threat, our liaison with allies and the messaging we think we need to get out to British nationals at any particular time.

“But it is very fast moving.



“The real solution to getting people out of this at volume is commercial airlines increasing their capacity and that capacity being available to British nationals.

“That’s a very significant part of our effort at the moment.”

The Foreign Office said it was anticipating further US and Israeli strikes – and warned Iran was not showing any signs of a slowing down or a reduction in its ability to fire missiles and drones across the region.

Oman’s capital can be reached by road from both Dubai and Abu Dhabi in journeys of about 300 miles.



British Airways said it will be operating one flight from Muscat, Oman, to London Heathrow, departing on Thursday.

The airline said: “We currently remain unable to operate flights from destinations including Dubai, Doha, Abu Dhabi, Bahrain, Amman and Tel Aviv.

“We’re continuing to do everything we can to support customers and colleagues in the region, and are in regular contact with them.



“We’re aware that a number of our customers are now in Oman, where the airspace is currently open.

“Working with the relevant authorities we’ve been able to schedule a flight from Muscat to London, departing at 2.30am local time on March 5.”

Oman Air has continued to operate its two return flights per day between Muscat and London Heathrow.

Emirates is operating two flights to Heathrow and one each to Gatwick and Manchester, all from Dubai.

At least some of these flights will use the airline’s A380 jets, which can each carry up to 615 passengers depending on how they are configured.



Emirates usually operates 146 weekly flights to the UK, which is the equivalent of about 21 per day.

Etihad Airways scheduled one UK flight on Tuesday, from Abu Dhabi to Heathrow, on a Boeing 787-10 Dreamliner with 336 passenger seats.

Qatar Airways, which usually serves Heathrow and Gatwick from Qatar, is running no flights because of the closure of Qatari airspace.

British Airways cancelled its timetabled flights to Heathrow from Abu Dhabi, Amman, Bahrain, Doha and Dubai on Tuesday.

The airline told passengers: “We are closely monitoring the situation and have cancelled a number of our flights to the Middle East.



“Safety is always our top priority and we would never operate a flight unless it was safe to do so.”

Passengers with bookings on certain routes up to March 15 are being allowed to amend the date to fly on or before March 29.

Virgin Atlantic cut a flight from Dubai to Heathrow.

About half a million passengers per day use airports in Dubai, Doha or Abu Dhabi, which are vital hubs for travel between Europe and the continents of Asia and Australia.

UK-based aviation consultant John Strickland said it could take weeks to clear the backlog of passengers.



RECOMMENDED STORIES

He said: “It’s a bit uncertain (when that will happen) because of course it will depend on how long the airspace restrictions remain in place.

“But factoring in the high volumes of normal daily traffic and the high capacity of aircraft such as the A380 and the Boeing 777, plus the fact that other flights covering similar routes operate at high occupancy, then it will certainly be quite a period of time which would likely run into weeks.”

News.title: EuroMillions results and numbers: National Lottery draw tonight, March 3
News.content:
GOT YOUR TICKETS? EuroMillions results and numbers: National Lottery draw tonight, March 3

Credit: Alamy

THE draw for tonight’s National Lottery EuroMillions (March 3, 2026) has taken place, with life-changing cash prizes at stake.

Check the results to see if you have just won a fortune and bagged enough to start that jet-set lifestyle you always dreamed of.



Every EuroMillions ticket also bags you an automatic entry into the UK Millionaire Maker, which guarantees at least one player will pocket £1million in every draw.



You can find out if you’re a winner by checking your ticket against tonight’s numbers below.

Tonight’s National Lottery EuroMillions winning numbers are: 06, 07, 24, 34, 50 and the Lucky Stars are: 05, 07.

The UK Millionaire Maker Selection: VHVZ15809.

Tonight’s National Lottery Thunderball winning numbers are: 10, 15, 16, 17, 24 and the Thunderball is 12.



TOP 5 BIGGEST LOTTERY WINS IN THE WORLD £1.308 billion (Powerball) on January 13 2016 in the US, for which three winning tickets were sold, remains history’s biggest lottery prize £1.267 billion (Mega Million) a winner from South Carolina took their time to come forward to claim their prize in March 2019 not long before the April deadline £633.76 million (Powerball draw) from a winner from Wisconsin £625.76 million (Powerball) Mavis L. Wanczyk of Chicopee, Massachusetts claimed the jackpot in August 2017 £575.53 million (Powerball) A lucky pair of winners scooped the jackpot in Iowa and New York in October 2018

The first EuroMillions draw took place on February 7, 2004, by three organisations: France’s Française des Jeux, Loterías y Apuestas del Estado in Spain and the Camelot in the UK.

One of the UK’s biggest prizes was up for grabs on December, 4, 2020 with a whopping £175million EuroMillions jackpot, which would make a winner richer than Adele.

Another previous UK winner who’s whole life was altered with their jackpot was a player who wanted to remain anonymous on October 8, 2019. They walked off with a cool £170,221,000.

Colin and Chris Weir, from Largs in Scotland, netted a huge £161,653,000 in the July 12, 2011.



Adrian and Gillian Bayford, from Haverhill, Suffolk, picked up £148,656,000 after they played the draw on August, 10, 2012, while Jane Park became Britain’s youngest lottery winner when she scooped up £1 million in 2013.

The odds of winning any EuroMillions prize are 1 in 13.

Could tonight’s jackpot of £152 million see you handing in your notice and swapping the daily commute for slurping champagne on a super yacht or lying back on a private beach in the Bahamas?

News.title: Fresh blow for PM Sir Keir Starmer after eco leftie Greens leapfrog Labour into second place in poll
News.content:
THE Greens have leapfrogged Labour into second place in a poll — dealing a fresh blow to PM Sir Keir Starmer.

Zack Polanski’s party rose four points in the wake of its Gorton and Denton by-election victory.





It now has 21 per cent of the vote and is just two points behind Reform’s 23 per cent, according to the YouGov survey.

Labour and the Tories are both on 16 per cent.

While it is just a single poll capturing the momentum of the Green Party’s win in Greater Manchester, it will set alarm bells ringing in Downing Street.

The PM is already under pressure from many of his MPs to adopt a more left-wing policy platform.



But No10 claims the Government is already pursuing a progressive agenda, including the pro-union Employment Rights Act and the scrapping of the two-child benefit cap.

A Green Party source told The Sun: “Gorton and Denton showed if people vote Green, they can get Green.

“Starmer can smear us as much as he wants, but the voters want change and increasingly see the Greens as the party to break the failed status quo.”

Sir Keir has called the Greens extreme and a risk to the UK.



Anthony Wells, of YouGov, said: “This is the highest we’ve had the Greens in a voting intention poll — the first time we’ve had them in second place.

“We need to see if this result is sustained in subsequent polls to evaluate the extent to which this effect sticks.”

News.title: Green Party’s idiotic student politics would ruin UK – but pathetic PM’s weakness makes Polanski look like a bold leader
News.content:
THE Green Party may have overtaken Labour in a new YouGov poll, but supporters shouldn’t hang out the bio-degradable bunting just yet.

Poll, schmoll. We’ve been here before (Neil Kinnock as a dead cert PM, anyone?) and, like so many supposed popularity surges, it proved to be just a sugar high when it came down to the meat and two veg reality of the electorate choosing someone to actually run the country.





But let’s indulge ourselves for a moment and take a proper look at the policies put forward by the party that is now supposedly just two percentage points behind Reform in popularity.

Not just the pro-Palestinian, anti-Israel stance that clearly helped secure the Muslim vote in the Gorton & Denton by-election, but the smorgasbord of other beliefs and values it might bestow on us.

Legalisation of all drugs, for starters. Nuclear disarmament and withdrawal from Nato, just as the world seems more volatile than ever.

Net zero emissions by 2040 (even faster than current targets), greater rent controls, amnesty to all undocumented illegal migrants, abolition of migrant detention except in cases of danger to public safety (how will they know until it happens?), full nationalisation of the five largest energy firms, railways and water companies, £48billion to the NHS within three years and, flying in the face of last year’s Supreme Court ruling, a commitment that self-declared trans women are women.

Oh, and a wealth tax to pay for it all.

Except that rich people have options, will no doubt leave, and the country will be worse off — as discovered by the many European countries who scrapped wealth taxes following a fiscal shortfall.

So it’s as fanciful as many of the promises that smack of ideological, student politics. As Succession’s Logan Roy might say, these are not serious people.

And they’re certainly not fit to govern 66million people and a £2.5TRILLION budget.

Moral intent is all well and good, but economic sustainability is critical and a Green revolution would prove a very expensive one, not least because the financial markets would panic and wipe billions from the economy.





So let’s take a deep breath and a large pinch of Himalayan sea salt before we believe the breathless nonsense about a “new political era” blah blah blah.

Wake-up call

In 2023, when now Green Party leader Zack Polanski was deputy, I appeared alongside him on Sunday With Laura Kuenssberg.

He’s a very nice man, and undoubtedly means well, so I can understand why younger voters in particular are drawn to him.

But when, on The Rest Is Politics podcast, he confused the UK’s national debt and deficit and didn’t know the top rate of tax, his attention to the important details appeared worryingly scant.

“I was horrified beyond belief on the economics stuff. It’s just not acceptable. If this is where British politics is going, no, no, no,” the podcast host and former Tory MP Rory Stewart said afterwards.

Indeed. But when the PM comes across as cautious to the point of paralysis, bold voices like Polanski’s can suddenly sound like conviction, bravery and leadership.

So let’s hope that, at the very least, this poll serves as a wake-up call to the other parties to get their houses in order and start acting like grown-ups.

That means Reform must sharpen up its policies and stop being a haven for disillusioned Tory MPs, the Tories need to show something different from the past 14 years of useless governance, the Lib Dems need to cut back on the gimmicks and Labour needs to find a new leader who will, er, show some leadership.

If they don’t, these little Green shoots might soon flourish in to the full bloom of a power to be reckoned with.



YES, I'M A KEEN LEFTIE LEFT-HANDERS have been revealed as “unusually competitive” in new research involving 1,100 adults. Alternatively, they could have just asked any of the unfortunate souls who have played a board game with me. Yes, I’m one of the ten per cent who’s “cack-handed” and, according to the research, more likely than right-handers to embrace competition as a route to self- improvement. Hmmm, whatever. Unlike some, I don’t see competitiveness as a character flaw – as long as its demands are restricted solely to yourself and go hand in hand with being a good loser. Admittedly, I write peculiarly – a tactic I had to adopt to avoid immediate smudging when forced to use a fountain pen at school. But otherwise, I rather enjoy being left-handed. Particularly when playing the mighty sport of rounders at school, when I would step up to bat holding it in my right hand and, as soon as the ball was bowled, immediately switch to my left and whack it far away from where the opposing fielders had misguidedly gathered. The trick only worked once per game, but it was enough to unsettle them and get our first rounder in the bag. Happy days. Competitive, moi?

Jessie Bucks trend…and is true Gwinner

AGE-DEFYING A-lister Gwyneth Paltrow was among those at Sunday’s Actor Awards (formerly SAG Awards) in the latest trend of a dress slashed to the waist.

Clearly, there was no sagging at the SAGs.

But, ironically, Irish actress Jessie Buckley once again stole the coveted Best Actress award (and the head-lines) whilst wearing a gorgeous gown that oozed old-school Hollywood glamour.

Proving that talent will always trump trends.

NEWS? I DON'T MISS IT ARRIVING back to the UK after a two-week, fully immersive adventure in Colombia was quite a shock. Not least because I switched off my data roaming and took a much-needed holiday from news flashes and doom-scrolling. So landing back at London Heathrow felt like plugging myself back in to the mains. Distance makes our national mood – the rolling outrage, the political party warfare, the certainty that this is the worst moment we’ve ever faced – feel faintly surreal when you’ve just spent time in a country emerging from decades under the cloud of drug lord Pablo Escobar and other warring factions. In 1993, he was tracked down and shot dead by Colombian national police, and in 2016 a peace deal was signed between the government and FARC (Revolutionary Armed Forces of Colombia) that formally ended more than 50 years of armed conflict. Ten years on, the country feels forward-thinking and celebratory, particularly through street art and music, while back here in supposedly progressive Britain, everything feels a little angry and regressive. But then perhaps that’s because I’m not “on holiday” here and, therefore, more switched on to the realities of daily life. Whatever. Perhaps the real gift of a holiday (if you can afford it) isn’t just an escape, but a reminder to occasionally unplug yourself from the constant noise of being hyper-informed.

IS IT SO PETRA-FYING?

DUBAI-based influencers finally have something to post about other than their latest beach outing or manicure.

Petra Ecclestone bizarrely wearing what resembled a row of cocktail sausages on her head, posted tearfully that she had experienced “one of the most scary, worst nights actually of my life”, after hearing explosions caused by Iran’s retaliatory strikes in the Middle East.

Former Love Islander Arabella Chi echoed the feeling of fear and talked of hiding her family in a store cupboard. It was enough to prompt me to call an old friend who moved there a couple of years ago.



“We’re fine,” she said. “It’s a little unsettling, but they’re intercepting missiles going over, so that’s what’s causing the damage. We’re not being targeted as such.

“The government has said it’s protecting us, has told us to stay indoors and keep calm, so that’s exactly what we’re doing.”

So while I appreciate that it must at times be scary, it was reassuring to hear this perspective rather than the scare-mongering amplified through social media.

News.title: Armed police swoop on Oldham Asda as 'aggressive man with weapon makes threats'
News.content:
Armed police swooped on an incident near an Asda store, after reports of an “aggressive male” feared to be armed with a knife.

Pictures showed armed police on Littlemoor Lane in Oldham following reports of a concerning incident close to the supermarket. Cops were called after a member of the public reported the man for allegedly making threats outside the store.

The caller reported seeing a handle which may have been part of a knife, prompting armed police to be called to the scene shortly before 1pm, the Manchester Evening News reports.

Ensure our latest headlines always appear at the top of your Google Search by making us a Preferred Source. Click here to activate or add us as your Preferred Source in your Google search settings.

Picture from Littlemoor Lane showed a number of police vehicles and a number of armed and unarmed police, who appeared to be concentrating on one address in the road.

No arrests have been made and no weapons have been seized.

A spokesman for Greater Manchester Police said: "At around 12.45pm on Tuesday 3 March 2026, we were called to reports of an aggressive male on Huddersfield Road in Oldham.

"Officers attended the scene and established that a male made verbal threats before making off. No arrests have been made at this stage, and investigations are ongoing."

News.title: UK facing urgent Iran terror warning and 'lone-wolf' attacks over Middle East war
News.content:
EXCLUSIVE: UK facing urgent Iran terror warning and 'lone-wolf' attacks over Middle East war

'Stuck between a rock and a hard place' nightmare for the UK as it faces a worrying choice between infuriating Donald Trump by not backing his Iran war - or raising the UK terror threat

View 9 Images US-Israeli war on Iran spreads to Beirut's southern suburbs (Image: Xinhua/Shutterstock)

Britain could face terror attacks from pro-Iranian terror networks and lone-wolf sympathisers, an Israeli intelligence expert has warned.

‌



Ex-Israeli forces intelligence officer Danny Citrinowicz said the UK is stuck between alienating President Trump by not getting involved - and suffering a greater terror threat. It came after the UK confirmed it would send a warship to the Middle East to protect UK interests.

‌



Asked if there was a risk to the UK, Citrinowicz said the UK faces terror threats from the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps and also Tehran regime sympathisers. He said: “Definitely the risk is there. And the risk will increase if the UK joins the war. And that is obvious.

‌



Keir Starmer deploys warship HMS Dragon to Cyprus as Iran crisis spirals READ MORE :

Former Defence Secretary Ben Wallace installed early favourite for vacant BHA role READ MORE :

View 9 Images Protests spread to Kashmir among Khamenei supporters (Image: Firdous Nazir/NurPhoto/Shutterstock)

Ensure our latest headlines always appear at the top of your Google Search by making us a Preferred Source. Click here to activate or add us as your Preferred Source in your Google search settings.

“You have the risk from the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps, who are in the UK and also from the elements living in the UK. They may feel bound to do something because of the so called decapitation of Khamenei. Definitely the risk is there and it will increase by joining the war.”

‌



Mr Citrinowicz, who is now senior researcher in the Institute for National Security Studies in Tel Aviv, added: “But the UK has to navigate, between that risk and the risk of President Trump taking action because you didn’t join the war.” But he added that apart from the threat of IRGC sponsored terror: “..more importantly, much more importantly is the lone wolf phenomenon, especially from people living in the UK.” It has long been suspected pro-Iranian regime sleeper cells loyal to or handled by the IRGC were in the UK and could launch attacks if called upon.

View 9 Images Destroyer Hms Duncan may deploy to region (Image: AFP/Getty Images)

Asked to specify that he means an actual terror attack he replied: “Yes, definitely.” The US-Israeli war on Iran has exploded further around the Middle East with major strikes on Iran and Lebanon - as the UK appeared to become further involved in the crisis.

‌



A £650m British warship will be deployed to the region to protect the UK military base at RAF Akrotiri, in Cyprus, from drone attacks launched by Iran or its proxies. The Type 45 destroyer HMS Duncan will be sent to the region, its crew having recently completed a training exercise on taking down drones. It comes after UK Prime Minister Sir Keir Starmer green-lighted the US using British bases for "defensive" strikes on Iran's missile sites, after US-Israeli strikes started.

View 9 Images Saudi Ras Tanura oil refinery attacked (Image: Satellite image Â©2026 Vantor/AFP)

A drone on Sunday hit RAF Akrotiri, a British sovereign air base on the island, though the Prime Minister said it was launched before his announcement and was not in retaliation. Shadow defence secretary James Cartlidge said HMS Duncan should "go now" because "we need the highly capable air defence it provides to protect RAF Akrotiri".

‌



He added: "It begs the question: why wasn't she already there?" Foreign secretary Yvette Cooper said the government had already added to the air defences in Cyprus, adding: "We have already increased the deployment, with additional defensive capabilities, including radar, F-35 jets, air defence, and counter-drone systems.”

View 9 Images Head to head: Donald Trump and Keir Starmer (Image: AP)

It comes as US Secretary of State Marco Rubio warned the “hardest hits” on Iran are “yet to come,” and amid concern US President Donald Trump’s war could last a long time. On Monday he claimed it may go on for ‘four to five weeks” but that is believed now to be an underestimate, despite sources saying Washington originally thought strikes would last days.

‌



It now appears the conflict, triggered by US President Donald Trump’s attack on Iran, is widening by the hour, with rising death tolls and fears of a long-term war. The US warned of an imminent attack in the Saudi City of Dharan, a major centre for the oil industry, as Tehran attempts to lash out further at the economy of the Gulf states.

View 9 Images Man shouts anti-US slogans in Tehran (Image: Morteza Nikoubazl/NurPhoto/Shutterstock)

And it has closed down its embassies in Saudi Arabia and Kuwait after drone attacks including one on its embassy in Riyadh as Iran retaliates across the Middle East. Saudi Arabia’s defence ministry confirmed the US embassy in Riyadh was hit by two drones, which sparked a fire and minor material damage to the building.

‌



A loud blast was heard and flames seen at the embassy early on Tuesday morning and black smoke was seen rising over Riyadh’s diplomatic quarter, which houses foreign missions. Tehran has already claimed to have closed down the key Hormuz Strait through which much of the world’s oil and gas supplies transit, saying it will “set on fire” any ships trying to pass. And President Donald Trump told Iran it's “too late” to talk and that its "air defense, air force, navy, and leadership is gone".

View 9 Images Iran attack: US Super Hornet takes off from USS Lincoln (Image: US Navy / SWNS)

Israel has yet again launched ground troops into southern Lebanon, warning locals to flee the region as it stepped up attacks on Iran’s proxy network Hezbollah. As well as major strikes on Tehran, including blasting the Presidential office, and throughout Iran Beirut was hit as Israeli warplanes demolished a key Hezbollah HQ.

‌



Death tolls throughout the region continued to pile up with the worst in Iran as 787 people are known to have been killed, including 176 children. The number of US service members killed is now six and deaths in Beirut come to 50, whilst there have also been casualties reported in the Gulf States.

View 9 Images Under attack: RAF Akrotiri in Cyprus (Image: Xinhua/Shutterstock)

The US has ordered all Americans to leave the Middle East in a cross-region alert as tens of thousands of Brits remain stranded on the fourth day of the war. The families of UK service personnel in Cyprus have been moved away from the base as a precaution against Iranian attack.

‌



On Monday, a spokesperson for the Cypriot government said two further unmanned drones heading towards the base had been intercepted. Mr Healey said the drone that struck Akrotiri was an example of the "dangerous and indiscriminate attacks" by Iran and its proxies.

View 9 Images Fighter jet over RAF Akrotiri as war spreads (Image: Xinhua/Shutterstock)

Cypriot government spokesman Konstantinos Letymbiotis spoke about the possibility of French support on Monday, telling reporters it could be "support similar to that of Greece".

Article continues below

French President Emmanuel Macron and Cypriot President Nikos Christodoulides had a phone call "in the context of strengthening the precautionary measures being taken", he said.

News.title: US commander tells troops Trump was 'chosen by Jesus to unleash Iran Armageddon'
News.content:
US commander tells troops Trump was 'chosen by Jesus to unleash Iran Armageddon'

A shocking report claims a US combat-unit commander claimed Donald Trump was 'anointed by Jesus to light the signal fire in Iran to cause Armageddon' after Saturday's attack

View 3 Images Trump speaking at the White House today (Image: SAMUEL CORUM/POOL/EPA/Shutterstock)

US troops have reportedly been told by commanders that Donald Trump was "anointed by Jesus" to attack Iran and "cause Armageddon".

‌



Trump announced Saturday that the US and Israel launched a joint operation and struck Iran, killing Iranian Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei. The attack was launched following Iranian nuclear negotiations and weeks of civil unrest in Iran.

‌



After the initial attack, Trump said "heavy and pinpoint bombing" would continue "uninterrupted throughout the week or, as long as necessary to achieve our objective of PEACE THROUGHOUT THE MIDDLE EAST AND, INDEED, THE WORLD!"

‌



The operation comes less than a year after Trump ordered strikes on Iranian nuclear facilities in June 2025, during which the President claimed to have obliterated Iran's nuclear capabilities. Trump told The New York Times this week that he ordered US troops to continue attacking Iran for "four to five weeks", without providing details of his plan.

View 3 Images A person stands on the roof of a building looking at a plume of smoke rises after a strike on the Iranian capital Tehran, on March 3 (Image: ATTA KENARE, AFP via Getty Images)

Ensure our latest headlines always appear at the top of your Google Search by making us a Preferred Source. Click here to activate or add us as your Preferred Source in your Google search settings.

‌



A fresh report found that hundreds of US troops across dozens of units and installations have submitted complaints to non-profit watchdog Military Religious Freedom Foundation (MRFF) since the attack, about combat unit commanders providing Christian reasoning for the war, independent journalist Jonathan Larsen reports on Substack.

Non-commissioned officers who attended a briefing Monday told the MRFF that a combat-unit commander "urged us to tell our troops that this was ‘all part of God’s divine plan’ and he specifically referenced numerous citations out of the Book of Revelation referring to Armageddon and the imminent return of Jesus Christ", Mirror US reports

The commander also reportedly argued that Trump was "anointed by Jesus to light the signal fire in Iran to cause Armageddon and mark his return to Earth" and that the Iran war is part of God's plan.

‌



View 3 Images The aftermath of Israeli and US airstrikes is seen at Nilufar Square (Image: Anadolu via Getty Images)

The remarks were submitted in more than 110 complaints about commanders across all military branches, including more than 40 units across 30 military installations, the MFRR told Larsen.

At least one complainant from an NCO identified themselves as a Christian, who could be deployed at any time to Iran, writing on behalf of 15 troops, including 11 Christians, one Muslim and one Jew.

Article continues below

In the NCO's Monday email, he warned that their commander’s remarks would "destroy morale and unit cohesion and are in violation of the oaths we swore to support the [C]onstitution".

MRFF President Mikey Weinstein, an Air Force veteran, told Larsen that his office has been “inundated” with such complaints, explaining: "These calls have one damn thing in freaking common; our MRFF clients [service members who seek MRFF aid] report the unrestricted euphoria of their commanders and command chains as to how this new 'biblically-sanctioned' war is clearly the undeniable sign of the expeditious approach of the fundamentalist Christian 'End Times' as vividly described in the New Testament Book of Revelation."

He added: "Many of their commanders are especially delighted with how graphic this battle will be zeroing in on how bloody all of this must become in order to fulfill and be in 100% accordance with fundamentalist Christian end of the world eschatology."

News.content:
21:47 Ethan Blackshaw

Trump says most people he wants to rule Iran are 'dead'

News.title: Inside £1bn HMS Dragon sent to Middle East - 72 missiles, radar Gatling gun, and 200 staff
News.content:
Inside £1bn HMS Dragon sent to Middle East - 72 missiles, radar Gatling gun, and 200 staff

The HMS Dragon was one of two Destroyer-class ships that was reportedly being considered by the Prime Minister to protect RAF Akrotiri after it was damaged by an Iranian drone

View 4 Images The Prime Minister has chosen to send the HMS Dragon to the Mediterranean (Image: Getty Images)

Keir Starmer has decided which of the advanced ships at the UK's disposal he will deploy toward the Middle East as the UK is dragged into Donald Trump and Israel's unilateral war on Iran.

‌



The Prime Minister has settled on the HMS Dragon, a £1 billion Destroyer class ship. The vessel will be sent to the Mediterranean after RAF Akrotiri in Cyprus was damaged, in what was believed to have been an Iranian Shahed drone incident.

‌



While the damage to the base's landing strip was deemed "limited" by Cypriot officials, the Government announced it would take a precautionary measure and send the ship to bolster regional security. The Dragon is one of the Royal Navy's most advanced ships, and was one of two believed to have been considered by Starmer ahead of the major decision today.

‌



Thousands of tourists trapped on UAE cruise ships as more explosions rock Dubai READ MORE :

British RAF F-35 jets shoot down drones in Middle East sparking huge explosion READ MORE :

Ensure our latest headlines always appear at the top of your Google Search by making us a Preferred Source. Click here to activate or add us as your Preferred Source in your Google search settings.

View 4 Images The Duncan and Dragon are two of the UK's Destroyer class ships (Image: PA)

The ship, according to the Navy, is "one of the most advanced in the world", and "instantly recognisable" thanks to the Welsh Dragon emblazoned on its bow that gives her the name. A Type-45 Daring class, she is 152.4m long, 21.2m wide, and boasts an 8,000 to 8,500-tonne displacement.

‌



The Dragon's primary role is to provide "protection to her fellow ships by detecting, interrogating and neutralising enemy threats", which it accomplishes using the "fearsome" Sea Viper anti-air missile system.

The system carries Sea Viper missiles, which the Navy boasts are capable of defending against even supersonic targets. She bristles with 72 missiles, CAMM launcher cells, and 4.5-inch (114 mm) Mark 8 naval, Phalanx CIWS - a type of radar-guided Gatling gun -, and 30mm guns. Dragon's massive frame and bristling arsenal is navigated by 200 personnel, who participate in long-range target acquisition, helicopter maintenance, and weapons maintenance.

View 4 Images Destroyer ships carry Sea Viper systems, which are deployed on the vessels' surface (Image: PA)

‌



Aside from being powerful in the conventional sense, the Dragon is also one of the few ships in the Royal Navy's massive fleet that boasts star power.

She famously made an appearance in No Time To Die, the 2021 Bond Film in which M, the fictional MI6 chief, orders a missile strike on a Japanese island being used by the villanous Safin to produce a dangerous bioweapon. Bond, played by Daniel Craig in his last dance with the character, is killed in the resulting firestorm.

The other ship considered by the Prime Minister, the HMS Duncan, is the sixth of the UK's Type-45 Destroyers, but isn't quite as advanced as its counterpart currently en route to the Mediterranean.

Article continues below

View 4 Images The Dragon is Hollywood famous for killing Daniel Craig's James Bond (Image: Universal Images Group via Getty Images)

The Duncan cost roughly £400 million to build, and is the same size and weight as the Dragon, while also carrying the same advanced Sea Viper system. Like the Dragon, it also comes with a Sampson radar, and boasts the same guns and missile capabilities - among them the ability to launch up to eight missiles in 10 seconds, and guide up to 16 for 70 mile stretches.

The ship is currently anchored at Portsmouth Naval Base, and, like its fellow Type-45s, participates in a range of missions ranging from defence against air attacks to providing humanitarian aid.

News.title: Martin Lewis causes website to crash after plugging free March legal service
News.content:
Martin Lewis causes website to crash after plugging free March legal service

Consumer guru Martin Lewis has encouraged everyone to ensure they have an up-to-date will in place, and well as power of attorney, during his ITV show on Tuesday night

View 2 Images Martin Lewis highlighted the offer of a free wills service in March (Image: ITV)

Consumer champion Martin Lewis caused a wills website to crash as he urged people to get their financial affairs in order.

‌



The founder of Moneysavingexpert.com dedicated the majority of his ITV show to the subject of wills and power of attorney. Part of the programme focused on Free Wills Month, which is happening during the whole of March.

‌



The initiative brings together a group of well-respected charities to offer members of the public aged 55 and over the opportunity to have their simple wills written or updated free of charge by using solicitors in selected locations across the UK. But after Martin highlighted it, the website freewillsmonth.org.uk was temporarily unavailable.

‌



Appointments with solicitors need to be booked by March 31, although Martin said: “Don’t leave it to the 31st, do it now because appointments get booked up, but your appointment could be after March.”

Ensure our latest headlines always appear at the top of your Google Search by making us a Preferred Source. Click here to activate or add us as your Preferred Source in your Google search settings.

View 2 Images Martin Lewis told viewers of his ITV show to prioritise getting power of attorney in place (Image: ITV)

‌



On the subject of why it is free, he said: “Because they hope you will leave a bequest to charity. That means you leave something in your will to charity, typically £300 to £1,000 or a percentage. It’s not compulsory but it is for charity and if you can, hey, once you’re gone you’re gone and you may as well leave them something.”

The star also used The Martin Lewis Money Show Live to go through what power of attorneys are and they are important. About 54% of people in a poll conducted ahead of the show said they had an up-to-date will in place but 24% have power of attorney.

On whether they or a will is more vital, he said: “Power of attorney is the most important. Without it, if you lose mental capacity your family could be locked out of your finances, even if you’re struggling to pay for your care or mortgage - a nightmare at the worst possible time.” He went on: “A will dictates what happens when you’re dead. A lasting power of attorney is for when you’re alive. f you lose mental capacity, don’t assume relatives can access you bank, your insurance and whole host of other things.

Article continues below

“That can literally mean the money you need to pay for your care if locked away and inaccessible. The money need to pay for your mortgage is locked away and inaccessible and someone else is going to have to find the cash in the meantime. That is why I say it is more important than a will.

“So consider, if you were to lose mental capacity, who would you like to look after you, who would you like to be able to access your funds to pay for things, who would you trust? And losing faculties doesn’t just happen to the elderly. Some people have severe strokes, have accidents, have early dementia.

“If you don’t have one in place it can cause real difficulties. You would need to apply to be a deputy via the court of protection. I rarely get good feedback on that. It can be costly, it can be stressful for families and it can take months and months.”

News.title: 'Donald Trump's Iran war planning template is just full of chaos and confusion'
News.content:
'Donald Trump's Iran war planning template is just full of chaos and confusion'

"US President Donald Trump's chaotic mixed messaging on his war on Iran have led to mass confusion about what the point of it is, beyond killing the leadership"

View 7 Images US President Donald Trump 'chaos and confusion' (Image: AP)

Even after Day 4 of unleashing hell on Iran, which has sparked a conflagration across the Middle East and beyond, Trump’s vision for the war is unclear.

‌



First, during last year’s June onslaught on Tehran’s atomic ambitions, it was all about stopping the Iranian regime from getting nuclear weapons in the ‘12 day war.’ This was a massive bombardment swiftly followed by “mission accomplished” messaging as Trump bragged the nuclear programme had been obliterated.

‌



He boasted: “Monumental Damage was done to all nuclear sites in Iran, as shown by satellite images. Obliteration is an accurate term! The white structure shown is deeply embedded into the rock, with even its roof well below ground level, and completely shielded from flame. The biggest damage took place far below ground level. Bullseye!!!"

‌



View 7 Images Ruins of a Tehran police station (Image: Morteza Nikoubazl/NurPhoto/Shutterstock)

View 7 Images Smoke rises over Tehran after strikes (Image: Anadolu via Getty Images)

He also said, displaying terrific ignorance of the difference between the two countries, that he believed Iran could follow Venezuela in a “perfect scenario” where most of the ruling government remained in power after a US operation captured Nicolás Maduro in January. And in the past few days he has switched, perhaps aware that his killing of the Ayatollah Khamenei was illegal, to launching the war because he was protecting the US and its allies from attacks by Iran.

‌



He said: "An Iranian regime armed with long-range missiles and nuclear weapons would be an intolerable threat to the Middle East, but also to the American people.”

View 7 Images Anti-war protests break out in New York (Image: Xinhua/Shutterstock)

Nobody disputes that the Ayatollah Khamenei’s regime was evil but it is unlikely to disappear with his death. And Trump made no mention of what Iran's future might look like after the war ends, or why he believed the country would no longer pose a threat to the US once this operation is completed.

‌



The remarks on Monday were a departure from his initial comments after launching the attack. Trump touted Khamenei's death over the weekend, but is yet to give a sense of what he believes the succession plan should be. With stunning idiocy he demolished any thought of having a plan as he boasted: "The attack was so successful it knocked out most of the candidates.

View 7 Images Security clampdown as far as Kashmir over war (Image: Saqib Majeed/SOPA Images/Shutterstock)

“It’s not going to be anybody that we were thinking of because they are all dead. Second or third place is dead." His hazy thinking and mendacity is so confusing it has even infected the clarity of his aides.

‌



In fact his positions have at times seemed at odds with other top administration officials, including Secretary of Defense Pete Hegseth. Hegseth, speaking on Monday just hours before Trump's remarks at the White House, rejected the idea that the US attacked Iran with the express goal of toppling the regime.

View 7 Images Trump's helpers: Vance, Rubio, Hegseth (Image: Getty Images)

He said: "This is not a so-called regime change war, but the regime sure did change.” Hegseth said Operation Epic Fury would be a success, but he did not offer details on the scope or duration of the conflict.

‌



It is almost pointless trying to find a purpose to the war because the longer it goes on it seems that the point is deflection, chaos and bluster which happens to kill lots of people. So far Israel has got what it wants, the death of Khamenei and gradual dismantling of the Iranian system. But it appears the Iranian regime is extending the war, lashing out at the entire region, knowing that it will become increasingly unpopular for the US.

View 7 Images Protests over Khamenei killing: Hyderabad (Image: Jan Ali Laghari/Pacific Press/Shutterstock)

Nobody expects a democracy to emerge from the ashes and it is likely the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps will remain intact, though depleted. A new Supreme Leader is being elected and they may pay lip service to the States as Trump is forced to withdraw whilst claiming a victory.

Article continues below

It will be a hollow one that has demolished trust in America. And it has further entrenched within the extremist community, and those who have joined them in the past few days, the belief that the west is evil.
